basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: ʿAbasa — “He Frowned” — bears a title unique in the Quran: it is the very action of the Prophet that gives the sūra its name. Tradition reports the occasion of the revelation: a blind man, Ibn Umm Maktūm, came to the Prophet while he was conversing with two Qurayshite notables — al-ʿAbbās and Umayya ibn Khalaf — whom he hoped to win for Islam. The Prophet, in order not to interrupt the conversation, frowned and turned away from the blind man. God then sent down this sūra, which opens with a reproach uttered in the third person — a delicacy avoiding frontal confrontation, says Qushayrī: “He said it by allusion, without addressing the Prophet directly.” Tradition reports that after this verse the Prophet never frowned again before a poor man, and that he even twice entrusted Medina in his absence to Ibn Umm Maktūm. The sūra then widens into a meditation on human ingratitude — for man, who was nonetheless drawn from a mere drop — on the benefits of creation, and closes on the image of the Day when each will flee his brother, mother, father, wife, and children. Qushayrī adds a splendid note: the gnostic is with creatures, but he leaves them with his heart.

versets.trad: He frowned and turned away
versets.commentaire: ʿabasa: he frowned. tawallā: he turned away. Qushayrī reports the cause: “The verse came down concerning Ibn Umm Maktūm, who was blind. He came to the Prophet while al-ʿAbbās ibn ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib and Umayya ibn Khalaf al-Jumaḥī were with him — the Prophet hoped for their conversion. In order not to interrupt the conversation, he turned away from the blind man and frowned. God sent down this verse.” And he adds: “There is delicacy in the language: God does not address the Prophet directly, but speaks of him in the third person. Then He addresses him personally.” The divine pedagogy proceeds in stages: one begins with the least hurtful.

versets.trad: because the blind man came to him.
versets.commentaire: al-aʿmā: the blind man. The Quran names him only by his handicap, as though the whole man were contained in his poverty. That is precisely what God defends: the worth of a man is not measured by his social power.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: What can tell you? Perhaps this man is purifying himself,
versets.commentaire: The verse switches to the second person: “you.” Qushayrī: “he would have become aware through what he learnt from you.” The blind man who was coming may have had in his heart a treasure the Prophet, in haste, did not see. And this is what the verse defends: do not judge a man's spiritual wealth by his social appearance.

mots.def: adrā: to make known; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: or enters a reflection that profits him?
versets.commentaire: yadhdhakkar: he remembers, he becomes aware. The reminder might benefit him. And behind the question, a wider truth: who knows for what spiritual appointment each comes — even though he has, in appearance, neither wealth nor rank?

mots.def: nafaʿa: to profit; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: subjunctive imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: As for the one who is well-off,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “The one who has dispensed with himself has dispensed with God. And it is said: he thought himself rich through his goods.” Istighnāʾ (deeming oneself self-sufficient) is the opposite of the spiritual path — the very root of iftiqār, acknowledged poverty (cf. sūra 96:7).

mots.def: istaghnā: to dispense with, to deem oneself independent.

versets.trad: you approach him eagerly;
versets.commentaire: taṣaddā: Qushayrī: “you turn your face toward him.” The reproach is precise: the Prophet did not only frown; he had turned toward the rich man. And it is this very movement that is noted.

mots.def: taṣaddā: to turn toward, to face with attention.

versets.trad: it matters little to you that he does not purify himself!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “You will not be taken to task if he does not purify himself — your task is only to convey.” The verse frees the Prophet from a responsibility that is not his: to convert is not in his power. But it also reminds: nor can you direct your care according to apparent chances of conversion.

versets.trad: But the one who comes to you full of zeal
versets.commentaire: yasʿā: he hastens, he makes the effort to come. The same root as saʿy in sūras 92 and 88. The one who toils to come to you — that is the one whose gesture deserves attention.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: saʿā: to make effort, to run.

versets.trad: and fearful,
versets.commentaire: yakhshā: he fears God. And that is what changes everything. Khashya is the very mark of the disposed heart. Where it is, spiritual attention must go — no matter the social rank.

versets.trad: you, you take no interest in him!
versets.commentaire: talahhā: you turn away in inattention, you let yourself be occupied with something else. Qushayrī sums up: “all of this is by way of admonition for the poor.” The sūra, through the Prophet, defends all the poor and all the simple seekers who come and are not heard.

mots.def: talahhā: to be distracted, to turn away.

versets.trad: No, truly! This is a reminder.
versets.commentaire: The subject shifts from the Prophet to the Quran. Tadhkira: reminder, remembrance. The Quran itself is a reminder — not a new message, but the awakening of a knowledge the soul already holds.

versets.trad: Whoever wills, will remember it.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives two readings that complement each other: (1) “The Quran is a reminder. Those whom God wills to remember will remember; those whom God does not will to remember will not remember — according to what the decree has established. There will be only what God has willed.” (2) “The statement is also by way of warning: let the one who wills to remember remember, let the one who does not will, not remember!” The two readings coexist — the first stating predestination, the second responsibility. The Quran carries both.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: It is contained in venerated pages,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “the pages of Abraham, of Moses, and before — and in the Preserved Tablet.” The Quran is the present echo of a knowledge eternally kept in heaven.

versets.trad: exalted, purified,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “elevated in rank and station, purified of all contradiction and lying.” Two qualities of the original Book: its heavenly rank and its absolute integrity.

versets.trad: in the hands of scribes
versets.commentaire: safara: Qushayrī: “the scribe angels.” The root s-f-r says embassy: the angels are the messenger-scribes between heaven and earth.

mots.def: safara: plural of sāfir; messengers, scribes.

versets.trad: noble and pure.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “noble with God, virtuous.” The Book passes through the purest hands before reaching the earth.

mots.def: barara: plural of bārr; pious, virtuous.

versets.trad: May man perish! How ungrateful he is!
versets.commentaire: qutila: Qushayrī, in a word: “may man be cursed — how great his ingratitude!” Passive imprecation (cf. sūra 85:4). Man receives so much — and he forgets. This ingratitude seals the gap between native dignity (the honoured pages) and human forgetfulness.

mots.def: qatala in the passive; here imprecation.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: akfara: to be ungrateful; exclamative form.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: From what matter did God create him:
versets.commentaire: Rhetorical question — that immediately recalls the humble matter from which man is drawn. Typical form of Quranic reminder: the proud man must return to his origin.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: from a drop of sperm. He created him and fixed his destiny;
versets.commentaire: nuṭfa: the drop of sperm. The exact word for naming the material origin of man. Qushayrī: “He created him and formed him and measured him by stages: from a drop, then a clot, then stage after stage.”

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.
perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: then He made the path easy for him;
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives two readings: “He has made the path easy for him in good and in evil, and has inspired him how to conduct himself.” And the second, natural and beautiful: “He has eased for him the going forth from his mother's belly — the head comes out first, upside down.” The verse says in a few words the whole of life: from the moral path to the path of the newborn body.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: then made him die and had him laid in the tomb;
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “He made a tomb for him so that wild beasts and birds would not devour him, and so that he would not be exposed to shame.” In the sūra, burial is a last favour: God protects even death from humiliation.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.
perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: then, when He wills, He will raise him again.
versets.commentaire: ansharah: He will resurrect him. The future awakening awaits His decree. Four stages in four verses: creation, path, death/burial, resurrection. The whole human life in four verbs.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: No, truly! Man does not accomplish what God orders him to.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “He disobeyed and opposed what was commanded to him.” Man received all that was needed — and he does not do what is asked of him.

mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.
perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: Let man consider his food.
versets.commentaire: And here comes the magnificent invitation: before waiting for cosmic proofs, look at your plate. The sūra is about to detail everything needed for a man to eat.

mots.def: naẓara: to look; in the energetic imperative.
mots.gram: energetic imperative verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: It is We who poured down the water in abundance.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī opens the allegorical reading: “in the allusive reading: We poured the water of mercy upon hardened hearts — and they softened for repentance. We poured the water of knowledge upon the hearts — and there grew in them the flowers of unicity (tawḥīd) and the lights of dispossession (tajrīd).” Material water that makes the grain grow; spiritual water that makes faith grow. One same metaphor for two levels of life.

mots.def: ṣabb: verbal noun, accusative of cognate object.

versets.trad: Then We cleft the earth deeply
versets.commentaire: Same verb as in sūra 84 al-Inshiqāq (the rending). The earth cleaves — not to swallow, here, but to let grow.

versets.trad: and We brought forth from it grain,
versets.commentaire: Grain: the most common food, the most essential.

versets.trad: vines and legumes,
versets.commentaire: qaḍb: Qushayrī: “fresh fodder.” A plant that is cut and grows again — fodder for beasts and green food for men.

versets.trad: olive trees and date palms,
versets.commentaire: The two blessed trees of Arabian Mediterranean: oil and date. And one has already met the olive as symbol of light (sūra 95).

versets.trad: lush gardens,
versets.commentaire: ghulb: Qushayrī: “dense and thick.” The gardens grow tight, like a head of hair.

versets.trad: fruits and pastures
versets.commentaire: abb: Qushayrī: “the pasture.” This rare word will return in the Quran only here. Tradition reports that ʿUmar, having to interpret the verse, said modestly: “Grain I know what it is, but abb...!” — and from that drew a lesson of humility before the text.

versets.trad: which you and your livestock enjoy.
versets.commentaire: matāʿ: provision for the journey, temporary enjoyment. All that grows, grows at once for men and for beasts. A common economy.

versets.trad: But when the great clamour comes,
versets.commentaire: al-ṣākhkha (الصَّاخَّة) — Qushayrī: “the Resurrection.” The word names the deafening noise, the cry that pierces the ears. Acoustic image of the end of times.

versets.trad: the Day when man shall flee his brother,
versets.commentaire: The sūra then opens the striking series of the five flights: brother, mother, father, wife, sons. All that made the fabric of life becomes, at that moment, what one flees.

versets.trad: his mother, his father,
versets.commentaire: The parents — first origin. Even them, on that Day, are fled.

versets.trad: his companion and his sons,
versets.commentaire: Wife and children — the future, posterity. Nothing more holds. But Qushayrī joins here the spiritual reading: “Let no friend of God (walī), no gnostic (ʿārif) be today without fleeing in his heart his brother, his mother, his father, his companion, and his sons. The gnostic is with creatures, but he leaves them with his heart.” And he then quotes the verses attributed to Rābiʿa al-ʿAdawiyya: « I have placed You in the heart as my interlocutor — and I have allowed my body to whoever wished to sit with me. » A splendid image: one can live with others, but not give them what only God should inhabit.

versets.ar: لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ
versets.tr: li-kulli mriʾin minhum yawmaʾidhin shaʾnun yughnīh
versets.trad: that day, to each man what concerns him will suffice.
versets.commentaire: shaʾn yughnīh: an affair that suffices him. Each, on that Day, is entirely absorbed by his own case. No more room for the other, even for the closest. And Qushayrī, transposing: “It is what they said: rectitude (al-istiqāma) is to witness the present moment as if it were the Resurrection.” To live now concentrated on one's matter with God, as if there were nothing else.

mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: That Day, there shall be radiant faces,
versets.commentaire: musfira: those that grow bright, as dawn (the root s-f-r is that of safīr, the dawn that lights the face of the earth).

versets.trad: smiling and joyful.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī, after evoking the joy of the just: “the reasons for their joy differ: some rejoice at having reached their Garden, some at their houris... and some because they have contemplated their Lord and seen Him.” Three stages of joy, as in sūras 83 and 88.

mots.def: mustabshir: who rejoices at good news.

versets.trad: And faces, that Day, shall be covered with dust,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “this is the dust of the transgressors (al-fussāq).” Visible mark of moral fall upon the face.

versets.trad: enveloped in darkness.
versets.commentaire: A prodigious verse in its concision. qatara: the blackness that covers. And Qushayrī, in six words, makes echo suddenly the sūra 83: “this is the humiliation of the veil (ḥijāb).” The worst chastisement is not the dust itself — it is the black veil that is added on top, separating forever from the Lord. As in verse 15 of sūra 83.

mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: These shall be the disbelievers, the libertines!
versets.commentaire: Closing verse. al-kafara al-fajara: the disbelievers and the libertines. The sūra, which had begun with a frown before a poor blind man, closes on the revelation of the true human face — between light and dust. And one hears, beneath the conclusion, the initial teaching: do not judge a man by his appearance; on the Day, it is the light or the dust of the heart that will appear, not his social rank.
